title: Dog Vomiting Blood Clots: Emergency Guide and Triage Advice
meta_desc: If your dog is vomiting blood clots, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why this happens and what steps to take for immediate veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Vomiting blood clots is a high-priority medical emergency indicating significant internal bleeding that requires immediate veterinary attention. Take a clear photo of the vomit if safe to do so, as this visual information helps the emergency team assess the severity and prioritize your dog’s care.

What does it mean if my dog is vomiting blood clots?

Vomiting blood, or hematemesis, is a serious clinical sign in dogs. When you see clots—which often appear as thick, dark red jelly-like clumps or resemble coffee grounds—it indicates that significant bleeding has occurred in the upper gastrointestinal tract. This can be caused by stomach ulcers, severe inflammation, or the ingestion of toxins and foreign objects.

Is my dog vomiting blood clots a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is considered a critical situation.
  • Immediate Danger: Internal bleeding can quickly lead to shock, anemia, or life-threatening complications.
  • Required Action: Contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • Wait Time: Do not wait to see if the symptoms pass, as early intervention is vital for a positive outcome.

How does taking a photo of the vomit help the veterinary triage process?

  • Visual Evidence: If you can do so safely, take a clear photo of the vomit to show the veterinary team.
  • Volume Assessment: These images allow the doctor to assess the total volume of blood loss and the specific color of the clots.
  • Source Identification: This information helps the clinical team determine the likely source of the bleeding.
  • Priority Care: Providing a photo helps the staff prioritize your dog’s treatment the moment you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, vomiting containing frank blood or having the appearance of coffee grounds suggests a bleeding lesion such as a gastric ulcer or neoplasm. The presence of blood clots in vomitus warrants immediate veterinary attention, potentially indicating a severe underlying condition. If serum iron is >300 mcg/dL along with clinical signs such as repeated vomiting and shock, or greater than the total iron binding capacity, chelation therapy may be necessary.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Infectious Disease

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 156)

faqs.question: What are common causes of blood clots in dog vomit?
faqs.answer: Common causes include gastrointestinal ulcers, ingestion of rodenticides (rat poison), severe parvovirus, foreign body obstructions, or blunt force trauma.

faqs.question: Should I try to induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting if your dog is already throwing up blood, as this can cause further damage to the esophagus and stomach lining.

faqs.question: What should I do while traveling to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and warm. Do not offer any food, water, or over-the-counter medications, as these may interfere with emergency diagnostics or surgery.
